title: Long READ block (no theme) — scrolled to bottom, footer-clear check

readBlocks.content:
Paragraph 1 — Now in Jerusalem, by the Sheep Gate, there is a pool which in Hebrew is called Bethesda, having five porticoes. In these porticoes lay a multitude of those who were sick, blind, limping, or paralyzed.

Paragraph 2 — A man was there who had been ill for thirty-eight years. When Jesus saw him lying there, and knew that he had already been a long time in that condition, He said to him, "Do you want to get well?"

Paragraph 3 — The sick man answered Him, "Sir, I have no man to put me into the pool when the water is stirred up, but while I am coming, another steps down before me."

Paragraph 4 — Jesus said to him, "Get up, pick up your pallet and walk." Immediately the man became well, and picked up his pallet and began to walk.

Paragraph 5 — Seems like a crazy question for Jesus to ask the man, doesn't it? Let us read it in context, understanding that Jesus fully understands the human condition.

Paragraph 6 — There are lots of other people with conditions like his there. This man's condition had become his identity, and his disability had become his excuse.

Paragraph 7 — Was it the water that healed any of them? Or was it something else entirely that the crowd had failed to notice over all those years of waiting?

Paragraph 8 — Consider how long thirty-eight years really is. An entire generation can be born, grow up, and start families in that span of time, and yet here he remained.

Paragraph 9 — The pool promised healing to whoever entered first when the water moved. It was a system that rewarded the strong and the quick, and punished the weak.

Paragraph 10 — But the man's deepest problem was not the pool, nor the crowd, nor even his legs. It was that he had stopped expecting anything to change at all.

Paragraph 11 — Jesus does not ask the question because He is unsure of the answer. He asks it so the man himself has to confront what he actually wants.

Paragraph 12 — Wanting to be healed sounds obvious, but healing means losing every excuse and every comfortable explanation built up over thirty-eight long years.

Paragraph 13 — Therefore if anyone is in Christ, this person is a new creation; the old things passed away; behold, new things have come into being in their place.

Paragraph 14 — The new creation is not a patched-up version of the old one. It is genuinely new, and that newness can feel uncomfortable and unfamiliar at first.

Paragraph 15 — To be made new is to re-learn how to walk, quite literally for this man, and figuratively for everyone who follows the same call out of the old life.

Paragraph 16 — Notice that the healing came before the man fully understood who Jesus was. Grace arrived first; comprehension followed later, as it so often does.

Paragraph 17 — He picked up the very pallet that had carried him in his sickness, now a sign of the freedom he had received rather than the prison he had endured.

Paragraph 18 — What is the pallet in your own life that you keep lying on, the familiar weight you could finally pick up and carry instead of being carried by it?

Paragraph 19 — The question echoes down the centuries to every reader: do you want to be healed, or have you grown comfortable explaining why you cannot be?

Paragraph 20 — Take a moment now, before moving on, to sit honestly with that question and to let it search the corners you would rather leave undisturbed.

★ END OF READING — this final line must be fully visible ABOVE the bottom navigation. If you cannot read this entire sentence, the footer is covering the text. ★
